title: Adjusting to Macroprudential Policies: Loan-to-Value Limits and Housing Choice
abstract: This study provides novel evidence regarding the effects of loan-to-value (LTV) limits on housing choices. Using a detailed loan-level dataset, I exploit the introduction of LTV limits in Israel. I find that the LTV limits led borrowers to choose housing units that were more affordable, farther from the central business district, and in lower socioeconomic neighborhoods. Additionally, these LTV limits increase interest rates and decrease loan amounts. The findings of this study indicate that macroprudential policies, which focus on the stability of the financial system, have micro implications on location choices, commuting costs, and movement to less-advantaged areas.

article.1 Background:
JEL G28 - Government Policy and Regulation R51 - Finance in Urban and Rural Economies Issue Section: Article Editor: Tarun Ramadorai Tarun Ramadorai Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

More than a decade after the global financial crisis, the drivers and implications of that event are coming into better focus. Mortgage lending and housing markets played central roles in the crisis, which led to lower consumer spending and diminished output growth, higher unemployment, and a slower recovery (Mian, Rao, and Sufi 2013;Mian, Sufi, and Verner 2017). As a result, policymakers in many advanced economies have implemented macroprudential policies (MPPs) to limit household leverage and thus slow the feedback loop between the credit market and home prices in order to reduce the likelihood of future crises. Nevertheless, despite the increasing adoption of MPPs, particularly in loan-to-value (LTV) limits, the transmission channels of LTV limits on housing choices have not been thoroughly explored. There has been, however, considerable empirical evidence of LTV limits reinforcing bank stability by reducing risks arising from borrowers caught in sharp housing price declines (Alam et al. 2019;Akinci and Olmstead-Rumsey 2018;Cerutti, Claessens, and Laeven 2017).

This paper examines the effect of financial regulation on housing choices and urban sprawl. Combining loan-level data that include borrower characteristics with a detailed property-level dataset, I examine the Bank of Israel’s 2010 and 2012 introductions of two types of LTV limits. Because of the LTV limits, affected borrowers purchased homes that were cheaper, smaller, and located in socioeconomically deprived neighborhoods, farther from Tel Aviv’s central business district (CBD). These MPPs focus on the stability of the banking system, with micro implications for the housing market. When homebuyers move farther from Israel’s center to lower-graded neighborhoods, there are implications on commuting costs, education levels, the labor market, and other economic factors.

The first MPP implemented in Israel in 2010 required commercial banks to increase the capital provision for mortgages with an LTV limit exceeding 60% (“soft LTV limit”), creating an implicit tax on lenders that originate loans with high LTV ratios. The soft LTV limit did not apply to housing loans initially amounting to less than 800,000 NIS (approximately US$200,000). The second policy, enacted in 2012, imposes LTV ratio thresholds according to buyer type (“strict LTV limit”): 75% for those acquiring a first home (“first-time homebuyers”), 70% for existing homeowners purchasing new units (hereinafter “upgraders”), and 50% for borrowers buying a home for investment purposes.

The LTV limits may affect the credit and housing choices of affected borrowers in a variety of ways. First, borrowers could choose not to borrow and postpone home purchases in order to save for a larger down payment. In this case, the proportion of renters could be expected to rise. I find no evidence that the homeownership rate or the percentage of renters changed following implementation of the LTV limits. Second, borrowers could decide to purchase the same housing unit and use their liquid financial assets to fund a larger down payment (Van Bekkum et al. 2019;Aastveit, Juelsrud, and Getz Wold 2020). However, I find no evidence of withdrawals from various financial assets after the limitations. Third, borrowers could decide to purchase the same housing unit using unregulated consumer credit to finance a larger down payment (Braggion, Manconi, and Zhu 2018). Lastly, borrowers can borrow less and fall below the margin, adjust their housing choices to the LTV limits, and purchase homes that are more affordable, smaller, and farther away from the CBD. This study finds evidence for the last two potential LTV adjustments.

Regarding credit choices, I provide evidence that the LTV limits increase interest rates and decrease loan amounts. For the soft LTV limit, using difference-in-differences (DiD) matching and an event study methodology, I find that affected borrowers paid a higher interest rate averaging 0.21–0.36 percentage points (pp) after the limitation. Thus, the soft LTV limit increased the annual payment, on average, by 2,700–3,250 NIS (4% of average household annual gross income). Although the policy is statutorily imposed on lenders, it appears that the majority of the economic burden is borne by borrowers in the form of higher interest rates.1The interest rate increase prompted some borrowers to reduce their leverage, especially high-income borrowers who were older and investors (who owned two or more homes). Overall, the soft LTV limit caused a decline in mortgage loans of approximately 2.4 billion NIS (4.7% of the annual mortgages in 2011). For the strict LTV limit, the limitation caused a decline in the LTV ratio to just below the cutoff values for each borrower type. Overall, the strict LTV limit caused the mortgage amount to decline by approximately 6.7 billion NIS (15% of the annual mortgage amount).

I argue that the increase in interest rates for high LTV loans primarily reflects the pass-through to borrowers of lenders’ increased origination costs. However, another interpretation is that the results indicate borrower selection (as determined byAcharya et al. (2022)in Ireland). In implementing soft and strict LTV limits in the Israeli context, credit rationing is not supported; that is, no significant change is observed in the distribution of borrowers’ observable characteristics after the imposition of the LTV limits.

To determine the impact of LTV limits on housing choices, I employ a DiD matching research design. My results show that because of the LTV limits, affected borrowers bought lower-quality assets: cheaper, smaller, farther from the CBD, and in lower socioeconomic neighborhoods. In the first six to 10 months following the imposition of the respective LTV limits, affected borrowers moved, on average, 4–7 kilometers (km) farther from Tel Aviv (a change in the distance from the CBD of 14%-15%), to significantly lower-rated neighborhoods. In terms of the distribution of distance from the CBD, nearly 20% of borrowers moved 5–10 km farther away and nearly 17% moved 10–20 km farther from the center. Overall, 70% of borrowers moved farther from the center of Tel Aviv. Moreover, within each subgroup of distance from the CBD, a greater proportion of borrowers lowered their socioeconomic standing by relocating to a less desirable neighborhood. According to Google Maps, moving 4–7 km away from the city increases daily commute time by 40 minutes to 1 hour.

Relative to the soft LTV limit of 2010, the strict LTV limit of 2012 had a much greater impact on borrowers’ choices in the credit and housing markets. This is likely because the strict limit did not allow borrowers the option of remaining highly leveraged. This is particularly true for investors, who changed their choices more than the other types of borrowers. This is consistent with the findings ofIgan and Kang (2011), who suggested that investors are more flexible in their purchasing decisions than first-time homebuyers, who usually have limits requiring them to purchase properties in specific locations, such as in close proximity to their parents or workplace.

Overall, a total of 15% of borrowers purchased homes farther from Israel’s center in the case of soft limits, and 14% of borrowers under strict limits. Under both soft and strict limits, 12% of borrowers moved to lower-ranking neighborhoods. After the 2012 limit, the borrowers reduced the size of their housing units by 8%, whereas after the 2010 limit, there was no significant change in the size of the housing units. I assume that the housing supply is inflexible, at least in the short term. I also examine only the choices in the housing market of the affected borrowers, a subset of buyers with a mortgage (which in turn is a subgroup of homebuyers).

The strict LTV limit yields counterintuitive results involving the credit market. Although the limits sought to reduce the risk of borrowers and thus improve their credit conditions, the results indicate that affected borrowers paid a higher interest rate and extended the length of their loans. Similar results were reported byDe Araujo, Barroso, and Gonzalez 2020, who found that banks may have altered their risk perception in response to a macroprudential supervisory authority’s signal about a buildup of systemic vulnerability among highly leveraged borrowers, which in turn affected banks’ risk pricing. This paper provides evidence for another possible explanation: Due to the strict limit, affected borrowers utilized unregulated forms of credit, such as consumer credit, thereby increasing their monthly loan payments.2In order to maintain the same monthly mortgage payment, affected borrowers were required to increase their term-to-maturity, which resulted in a higher mortgage interest rate.3As consumer credit in Israel is not subject to the same scrutiny as mortgages, it may pose new threats to the financial system.

This study contributes to the expanding literature on MPPs by estimating the impact of LTV limits, the most common MPP tool, on borrowers’ choices in the credit and housing markets. It is one of the few studies to jointly analyze mortgage credit, housing, and borrower characteristics to demonstrate that MPPs influence borrowers’ housing market choices (e.g.,Igan and Kang 2011;Han et al. 2021). The majority of micro-level research on the effects of MPPs has focused on lender responses (e.g.,Acharya et al. 2022;DeFusco, Johnson, and Mondragon 2020). Recent research on the causal effect of MPPs on housing prices found a small effect (Duca, Muellbauer, and Murphy 2011;Laufer and Tzur-Ilan 2021;De Araujo, Barroso, and Gonzalez 2020), but with mixed evidence, whereas others found no effect (Kuttner and Shim 2016). This is one of the few studies to examine the impact of an LTV limit on borrowers’ choices in the housing market, not only in terms of price but also with regard to asset size and location (see alsoAcharya et al. 2022). As LTV limit is an optional tool to target speculator activity in the housing market, this paper is also related to the extensive literature on the role of speculators in fueling housing market booms and busts (Chinco and Mayer 2016;Haughwout et al. 2011;Bayer et al. 2011).4
1.1 Macroprudential policies implemented in Israel

The global financial crisis did not markedly affect Israel. However, the inability of Israel, as a small and open economy, to disassociate itself from low global interest rates contributed to a trend of rising asset prices, especially housing values. From 2007 to 2016, nominal house prices in Israel increased by 118%, real house prices increased by 84%, and the volume of mortgage debt increased by 93%, causing concern among policymakers. In response, between 2010 and 2014, Israel, like many other advanced economies, implemented a series of MPP tools.5

The timeline and descriptions of each of the five MPP tools implemented are provided inTable 1. The rapid succession of five different MPPs was primarily intended to affect housing demand. In the words of the governor of the Bank of Israel, Stanley Fischer: “We have imposed many measures, and they have not yet done the job. Evidently, the measures we have imposed have not been strong enough to sufficiently reduce the demand for housing to stop prices rising” (Akerlof et al. 2014).

This study also examines the brief periods preceding and following each LTV limit (shown in red inFigure 1), in order to differentiate the LTV limit effects from those of other MPP tools implemented around the same time. 1.2 The LTV limits

1.2.1 Soft LTV limit

The soft LTV limit was the first MPP to be implemented. The Israeli supervisor of banks issued a directive in October 2010 requiring banks to increase capital provisions to 100% for mortgages with variable interest rate portions above 25% and LTV ratios greater than 60% (from the previous 35%–75% depending on loan characteristics).7Importantly for the present analysis, the guidelines applied only to mortgages greater than 800,000 NIS (approximately US$200,000). I focused on the first red area inFigure 1, between January 2010 and May 2011, prior to the implementation of the second MPP tool (“Variable Interest Rate” inFigure 1).8

1.2.2 Strict LTV limit

The strict LTV restriction took effect in November 2012. It established that a bank could not approve a mortgage with an LTV ratio exceeding 75% for first-time homebuyers, 70% for upgraders (who must sell their existing home within 18 months), and 50% for investors (who own two or more homes). For the strict LTV limit analysis, I focus on the period between January 2012 and September 2013 (the second red area inFigure 1), before the implementation of another MPP (“PTI+Duration”).
article.2 Data:
2.1 Data construction

This study utilizes loan-level data supplied by the Bank of Israel, which contains information on all mortgages issued by the seven commercial banks in Israel. These lenders account for approximately 95% of all mortgages in Israel. The dataset contains data on loan characteristics: date of mortgage issuance, date of housing asset purchase, loan amount, interest rate, maturity, and loan-to-value (LTV) ratio. Roughly half of the data include the location and value of acquired property in terms of home characteristics. Borrower age and monthly income are also included in the data and available for all observations.

I merged the loan-level dataset with CARMAN data provided by the Israel Tax Authority to learn more about the effect of LTV limits on housing choices. The CARMAN database contains details on all home sales and their characteristics (seeAppendix Afor a description of the merging process). Approximately one-third of mortgages were successfully matched with the CARMEN data. I explain inAppendix Ahow the merger did not result in any biases.

2.2 Summary statistics

The dataset is divided into two periods: before and after the imposition of each LTV limit. For the soft LTV limit, the numbers of observations before implementation (January 2010 to October 2010) and after (to May 2011) are 16,100 and 11,224, respectively. For the strict LTV limit, the numbers of observations before (January 2012 through October 2012) and after (through August 2013) are 17,260 and 16,761, respectively.Table 2shows the descriptive statistics for mortgage contracts, borrower characteristics, and home purchase transactions before and after the imposition of the soft LTV limit. These illustrative statistics reveal some of the primary results. After the soft LTV limit, the average interest rate increased while the average LTV ratio decreased. After the LTV limit, borrowers purchased less expensive assets, that were smaller (not significantly) and farther from Tel Aviv.

This paper focuses on Tel Aviv, which is Israel’s only CBD. The Israel Central Bureau of Statistics defines the center of Israel between two coastal cities, Hadera and Gedera. Hadera is at the northern end of central Israel, whereas Gedera is at the southern end. Hadera and Gedera are 80 km apart, and Tel Aviv lies between them. Therefore, I limit the distance to 40 km from Tel Aviv, where 55% of Israel’s population resides.Figure 7illustrates this through the Israeli population distribution. The majority of businesses in Israel are located in the Tel Aviv metropolitan area, and virtually the entire tech industry is located within the city of Tel Aviv. Furthermore, the Tel Aviv metropolitan area is considered the economic center of Israel and accounts for more than 50% of the country’s GDP. Therefore, the CBD model can be implied to Tel Aviv, where prices decrease with distance from the city.9

The Israel Central Bureau of Statistics publishes a socioeconomic index based on 16 variables, including demography, education, employment, income, and standard of living. Based on this index, all neighborhoods in Israel are classified into one of 20 clusters, with 1 representing the lowest socioeconomic status and 20 representing the highest.Table 2reveals that borrowers purchased homes in neighborhoods with lower rankings after the imposition of the soft LTV limit.

In Israel, investors are those who own two or more homes. They will be automatically classified as an investor by the Israel Tax Authority (and will be required to pay housing tax in accordance with investor taxes). When purchasing a home in Israel, one must affirm in the presence of a lawyer the number of properties held. The Israel Tax Authority also keeps track of the number of properties each individual owns and can easily monitor any changes.

Table 3provides a summary statistics of mortgage contracts, borrower characteristics, and home purchase transactions prior to the implementation of the strict LTV limit for each borrower type: first-time homebuyers, upgraders (those who must sell an existing home within 18 months), and investors (who own two or more homes).Appendix BandTable A.2contain the summary statistics for leveraged borrowers before the implementation of the strict LTV limit: first-time homebuyers, upgraders, and investors with LTV ratios exceeding 75%, 70%, and 50%, respectively. Overall, leveraged borrowers account for 19% of borrowers. Of the total, 12.4% of first-time homebuyers and 12.3% of upgraders had LTV ratios that exceeded the new limits prior to implementation, while 60.1% of investors exceeded the new limits.Appendix Bdemonstrates that affected borrowers of each type bought less expensive houses, smaller, farther from the center, and in lower-graded neighborhoods, than the average borrower.

article.3 First-Stage Effects of LTV Limits on C:
The soft LTV limit compelled banks to reserve additional capital against risky loans. This section examines whether the limit was binding and the banks’ responses to this policy.Figure 2demonstrates that banks increased the average interest rate charged to risky borrowers, defined as those with an LTV ratio greater than 60%. However, after the policy took effect, borrowers and macroeconomic factors, such as interest rates, may have changed.

Fig. 2 Change in average mortgage interest rate for risky borrowers (loan-to-value > 60%) This graph depicts the average mortgage interest rate for mortgages with LTVs greater than 60% for two time periods: before the soft LTV limit, between January 2010 and October 2010 (N = 16,100), and after the policy, between November 2010 and May 2011 (N = 11,224). In both figures, an Epanechnikov kernel distribution with a bandwidth of 0.0606 is used. According to a Kolmogorov-Smirnov test (p < .001), the two distributions are statistically distinct. Source: Bank of Israel mortgage data. Open in new tab Download slide

Next, I compare the interest rates paid by two similar borrowers (matched by income, age, bank, and loan duration) just above and below the 60% LTV limit (61% vs. 59% in the first test and 61%–65% vs. 55%–59% in the second test). Prior to the regulation, there was no significant difference between the interest rates paid by borrowers above and below the 60% LTV threshold (with a 0.01–0.03 pp difference in their interest rate). However, after the LTV limit, the interest rate paid by a borrower with an LTV just above 60% was 0.21–0.36 pp higher than the interest rate charged to a borrower with a comparable LTV just below the LTV limit (Table 5). The results of a quasi-experimental approach based on event studies surrounding the implementation of the soft LTV limit are presented inAppendix C. I obtained similar results.

The soft LTV limit increases annual interest rate payments, by an average of 2,700 to 3,250 NIS (4% of average household gross yearly income). Thus, despite the fact that the policy is statutorily imposed on lenders, it appears that borrowers bear a significant portion of the economic burden in the form of higher interest rates.10

The increase in interest rates encouraged borrowers to reduce their leverage.Figure 3depicts the LTV distribution before and after the soft LTV limit with two peaks, one at approximately 75% and the other at 60%. In response to the soft LTV limit, some borrowers maintained high leverage (and paid a higher interest rate), while others reduced their leverage to below 60%. At the same time, it is also possible that the credit-constrained borrowers had no choice but to stay highly leveraged and pay a higher interest rate.Appendix DandFigure A.3show that credit-constrained borrowers (i.e., lower-income borrowers, younger households, and first-time homebuyers) chose to remain highly levered and pay a higher interest rate. Meanwhile, those who chose to lower their leverage are mainly higher-income borrowers, especially investors and upgraders.11The bunching method is utilized inAppendix Gto estimate the effect of the soft LTV limit on the mortgage amount. The decline in total mortgage volume was approximately 2.4 billion NIS (about 4.7% of mortgage originations in 2011).

3.1 Credit rationing

An important question in the literature usually arises from changes in credit conditions: Does imposing an LTV limit affect the distribution of borrower characteristics? Are the same types of borrowers present before and after the imposition of the restriction, or does the LTV limit push out certain types of borrowers—perhaps those who are more credit-constrained? During the phenomenon known as “credit rationing” (Stiglitz and Weiss 1981), banks limit the supply of additional credit to borrowers based on their characteristics, even if the borrowers are willing to pay higher interest rates.

I examine the distribution of borrower age and income prior to and following the implementation of soft and strict LTV limits (Figure 5: panels A to D). KS tests show no statistically significant change in the distribution of borrower age and income (p-values: .58, .72, .60, and .48). I also examine the change in borrower activity in the credit and housing markets before and after the strict LTV limit (Figure 5: panels E and F). The vertical line indicates when the strict limit was imposed. A KS test shows no significant change in borrower activity in the real estate or mortgage markets following the restrictions (p-values: .19, .24).12

article.4 Identification Approach:
4.1 Soft LTV limit: difference-in-differences matching

The dataset contains no information about household decisions before and after the implementation of the soft LTV limit; it represents a single point in time. Consequently, the challenge is locating and comparing households with similar characteristics before and after the limit imposition, as well as comparing their credit and housing market choices.

Let Yi represent the decision made by the borrower (i), such as the price, size, or location of the housing unit. Let Ti represent the treatment defining all households after October 2010 as being subject to the treatment—that is, Ti=1. The average treatment effect (ATE) is useful for evaluating the expected impact of randomly assigning individuals in the population to a treatment on the outcome. The naive estimator will therefore be:

Y 1 = E ( Y i | T i = 1 ) − E ( Y i | T i = 0 ) (1)

Abadie and Imbens (2011)noted that the ATE may not be applicable to policymakers because it includes the previously mentioned effect on individuals for whom the program was never intended. The ATE on the treatment group (ATT) is useful for evaluating the aforementioned effect on the intended recipients of the program. Consequently, I will use the ATT as the variable of interest in this study.

Credit and housing choices are not random decisions but are correlated with household characteristics. The matching method helps solve this problem by assigning each observation in the treatment group to the closest observation in the control group, based on observed characteristics. I focus on the ATT parameter for an individual with characteristics X:

ATT = E ( Y 1 − Y 0 | T = 1 , X ) (2)

where Y1 and Y0 are the outcome variables for the treated and untreated households, respectively. For each i, matching estimators impute the missing outcome by finding in the data other individuals whose covariates are similar but who are exposed to the other treatment. In this way, differences in outcomes between this well-selected and, hence, adequate control and the treatment groups can be attributed to the treatment (Abadie and Imbens 2006;Abadie et al. 2004).13

The soft LTV limit required banks to increase capital provisions only for mortgages exceeding 800,000 NIS. Consequently, I am able to divide the sample into treatment and control groups. The main challenge here is choosing groups far from the cutoff to avoid overlap between them, but not so far that they differ vastly in terms of observable characteristics. In the baseline estimation, I examine two groups of borrowers: those who borrowed 600,000 and 700,000 NIS, just below the threshold (the untreated group), and those who borrowed 900,000 and 1,000,000, just above the threshold (the treatment group).Figure 6demonstrates that the soft LTV limit significantly altered the distribution of LTV ratios in the treatment group, while the distribution of LTV ratios in the control group remained unchanged.

Then, the matching process is compared between the treatment and control groups. Thus, the ATT now is calculated as

ATT = ( After − Before ) treated − ( After − Before ) control . (3)

The treatment group consists of 1,498 observations (844 and 654 observations before and after the LTV limit imposition, respectively), while the untreated group consists of 3,462 observations (2,023 and 1,439 observations before and after the LTV limit imposition, respectively). The control group consists of 1,498 observations that are matched to the treatment group by observable characteristics (895 observations before the LTV and 603 after). The characteristics of the two groups before and after the matching process are presented inTable 4. Before the matching process, borrower characteristics differ significantly between groups; however, after the matching process, observed differences between groups are insignificant.

4.2 Strict LTV limit: Predicted LTV ratio using DiD matching

This study focuses on the strict LTV policy’s effect on the subset of borrowers constrained by the limit. However, the treatment status can be observed only before the policy intervention. After the intervention, it is difficult to differentiate between affected and nonaffected borrowers. Hence, the identification challenge in the case of the strict LTV limit, compared with the soft LTV limit, is having no clear treatment and control groups. As suggested byAbadie (2005), one way to determine the treatment status of the post-program sample is to use “some individual characteristic observed in both periods.”

The DiD matching approach identifies the affected borrowers using a prediction of the LTV ratio that the borrower would have chosen without the limitation. By this method, only households (slightly) below the cutoff after the policy are examined, and the closest household from the period before the policy is matched based on observed characteristics (age and income). Then, only two groups remain: households that chose the same LTV ratio before and after the policy (the control group), which is slightly below the cutoff, and households that chose to be above the LTV cutoff before the policy (the treatment group).14Age and income are among the many factors that can affect housing choices.15InAppendix E, I added more borrower characteristics for a small subset of borrowers, and the results were comparable to the baseline estimation.

The outcomes in the empirical application are the mortgage outcomes (interest rate, maturity, and default rate) and the characteristics of the housing unit (price, size, location, and neighborhood socioeconomic level). I use theAbadie and Imbens (2011)estimator, where the ATE on the treatment group (ATT) for an individual with characteristics X (age and income) is:

ATT = E ( Y 1 − Y 0 ) | T = 1 , X ) (4)

Here Y1 and Y0 denote the outcome variables for households receiving treatment and those not, respectively. After the matching procedure, I examine the difference between the choices of the borrowers in the treatment group and those in the control group, using the DiD method. This method compares the outcomes of two groups: one group above the LTV cutoff and the other below the LTV cutoff (treated and control groups, respectively), both before and after the LTV limit. Consequently, the ATT is calculated as ATT = (after/before) treatment group – (after/before) control group. In addition, it is calculated separately for each outcome variable and borrower type.
article.5 The Effect of LTV Limits on Housing Ch:
5.1 Soft LTV limit

The soft LTV limit increases interest rates and decreases loan amounts, leaving some with less credit for the purchase of a home. Before and after the implementation of the soft LTV limit,Table 6compares the housing choices of the treatment and control groups (Equation 3). In addition,Table 6demonstrates that due to the LTV limit, affected borrowers purchased less expensive homes, a decrease of 68,000 NIS (4.7%), compared with the control group. I did not find a statistically significant effect of the LTV limit on the size of the housing units, in terms of square footage or number of rooms. In terms of distance from Tel Aviv, the results indicate a significant distance of 4.3 km (14.7%) from the city for the treatment group compared with the control group.16The DiD matching results also indicate that treated households moved to significantly lower-graded neighborhoods: After the LTV limit, affected borrowers purchased assets in lower socioeconomic neighborhoods, which is 2.2 points lower (12.3%) in terms of neighborhood quality relative to the control group.

The soft LTV limit identification strategy has a number of limitations. It is not a typical difference-in-differences design because borrowers self-select themselves into treatment and control groups based on their borrowing choices (loan amount). In theory, there are factors other than age and income that influence treatment assignment, such as wealth, financial knowledge, and even bargaining power. These unobserved differences among borrowers could be correlated with housing choices and could bias the results. In addition, other potential sources of variation that could introduce bias into the estimates are shocks correlated with loan size, which determines treatment assignment and housing choice. For instance, an unexpected change in wealth or the birth of a child could result in a change in the loan amount and a shift in housing choices. Age and income are the only observable characteristics I have access to in my data. As a robustness test inAppendix E, I merged the data with another dataset containing additional household characteristics, such as socioeconomic indicators, and the results were robust—perhaps because those unobservable are correlated with age and income. Another possible mechanism is the control group outbidding the treatment group and displacing them from urban centers. For instance, lenders may respond to the increase in risk weights by issuing more low-LTV mortgages to the control group, whose members may be more affluent; these individuals may then purchase homes closer to the center. Consequently, the treated group, which may be more credit-constrained, must relocate to the periphery. A major limitation of the data in this paper was an inability to use any lender-side information to explore these relevant questions.

Real estate markets experience systematic above-trend increases in prices and transactions during the spring and summer (“hot season”), but decreases below-trend during the fall and winter (“cold season”) (Ngai and Tenreyro 2014). My analysis focuses on the few months preceding and following the October 2010 restriction. To account for seasonality in the real estate market, I selected comparable months preceding and following the limit. In this instance, however, if I selected the same months before and after the limitation (i.e., January to October), I would need to account for another MPP implemented in May 2011 (described inTable 1as “Variable interest rate”), whose effects I wanted to separate from the LTV limit. Consequently, I selected January–May 2010 as the pre-policy period and compared it with the post-policy period (January–May 2011). The results inAppendix Findicate that the main effects of basic estimation remain significant when seasonality is taken into account.Table A.4(first row) demonstrates that affected borrowers during this period purchased houses that were cheaper, farther from the center, and in lower-ranking neighborhoods. Choosing these dates controls for seasonality, but has a significant drawback: The time window is now far removed from the policy implementation date, which makes it difficult to reliably attribute the results to the limitation and not to concurrent events. Consequently, I included a placebo test examining similar dates a year prior to the policy’s implementation (i.e., January–May 2010 compared with January–May 2009). The results in the second row ofTable A.4indicate that there is no significant effect at this time.

Figure 7depicts the Israeli population distribution to highlight the magnitude of the baseline estimation results in the context of Israel. Overall, 55% of the Israeli population resides in the center of the country, within a 40-km radius of Tel Aviv. During the first six months after the implementation of the soft LTV limit, affected borrowers moved, on average, 4.3 km (14.7%) farther from Tel Aviv and into significantly lower-graded neighborhoods. I also examine the distribution of the change in distance from the center using loan-level data. Although the average distance moved from Tel Aviv was 4.3 km, nearly 20% of borrowers moved 5 to 10 km, and nearly 17% moved 10 to 20 km. Overall, 70% of all borrowers moved farther from the city center.17

An intriguing question is whether the socioeconomic level of neighborhoods improved for borrowers who relocated farther away from Tel Aviv.Figure 8depicts the distribution of neighborhood grading changes by distance from Tel Aviv, categorized into three groups: upgrade (gray), downgrade (blue), and no change (red). Within each subgroup of distance from Tel Aviv, statistically significantly more borrowers moved to lower-graded neighborhoods than to higher-graded areas. The evidence suggests that MPPs, which focus on the financial system’s stability, can have micro implications on location choices and, consequently, on commuting costs. Moving to disadvantaged areas may have long-term effects on the mental and physical health of adults and the educational attainment and earnings of children, according to a large body of research (Chetty, Hendren, and Katz 2016;Chetty and Hendren 2018;Kling, Liebman, and Katz 2007;Ludwig et al. 2012).

Appendix Hexamines the differential effect of the soft LTV limit on population subsegments. This test determines whether LTV limits make it difficult for credit-needy households to purchase homes. I find that older adults (Table A.6) and investors (Table A.7) were the most affected by the soft LTV limit and altered their housing choices the most. These results may be attributable to the possibility that older adults and investors are more adaptable purchasers. In contrast to younger adults or first-time homebuyers, who may face different limitations and constraints requiring them to purchase properties in specific locations, such as those with proximity to parents or work (similar results were obtained byIgan and Kang (2011)), older adults and investors can either delay housing purchases or compromise on the type of asset.

5.2 Strict LTV limit

Recall that we do not know what the borrowers’ leverage choices would have been if the policy intervention had not occurred; only their actual leverage choices are known. Consequently, we use borrower characteristics (age and income) and leverage choices prior to the policy to infer their leverage choices subsequent to the policy using the matching method. For instance, suppose that younger and lower-income households were more likely to exceed the LTV threshold prior to the implementation of the policy for each buyer type. In essence, the empirical exercise compares younger and lower-income households to other households before and after the implementation of the policy. To prevent significant differences in observable characteristics between the treated and control groups, the empirical strategy focuses on a narrow range of LTV ratios across policy cutoffs.

Tables 8and9display the results of the DiD matching of housing and credit choices between the control and treatment groups before and after the policy. The results are presented in absolute terms inTable 8, whereas the rate of change in their preferences is shown inTable 9. After the strict LTV limit was imposed, affected borrowers purchased homes that were less expensive, smaller, located farther from the city center, and in lower-graded neighborhoods. In each instance, investors altered their choices more than the other two groups.18

Moreover, the strict LTV limit introduced in 2012 (as opposed to the soft LTV limit introduced in 2010) had a much greater impact on borrower choices in the credit and housing markets. This is especially true for investors who purchased property farther from the center (24%), property in lower-graded neighborhoods (18%), and smaller houses (14%), compared with 9%, 15%, and 2%, respectively, in response to the soft LTV limit. This is likely due to the strict LTV limit for investors, which is set at 50%, thus affecting 60% of investors.

In response to the strict and soft LTV limits, borrowers moved to less expensive properties and changed their housing location choices, away from their previously desired area. These results are an alternative to those presented in the models ofStein (1995)andAndersen et al. (2022), and in evidence inBernstein and Struyven (2022), in which more expensive mortgage credit increases the necessary downpayment to ensure the same quantity and location of housing consumed while keeping the monthly mortgage payment fixed. The leading force in these models is that households are reluctant to downsize, prompting them to list their existing houses at higher prices, leading to a “housing lock” in the market. This paper shows that while downsizing aversion may exist, it does not rule out downsizing as a margin of adjustment: In the case of the 2012 limit, borrowers reduced the average size of the homes they purchased. However, the soft LTV limit did not cause borrowers to purchase smaller homes, in terms of neither square meters nor rooms. Overall, the most significant change in housing choices after both strict and soft LTV limits is the location of the housing unit, which raises the question of why borrowers in Israel are more flexible in their location choices but not in their house size preferences. It is less surprising in the Israeli context, where the average (nonreligious) family has 3.2 children.19Consequently, the demand for larger housing units, with four to five rooms, has remained stable over the past few decades. The supply side is another possible explanation for the inelasticity of house size. In Israel, municipal authorities exert substantial influence over the availability of land within municipal borders (Eckstein, Tolkovsky, and Tzur-Ilan 2012). The municipal rates payment is determined by multiplying the size of the property by the per-square-meter rate set by the local authority council. In contrast to the method used in Israel, the property tax in the majority of the world is calculated by multiplying the legal rate by the property’s value. Consequently, local governments are constructing larger housing units, which is also stable over the past few decades. Overall, it appears that both the demand and supply for housing units in Israel are inelastic with regard to the size of the housing units.

Appendix IandTable A.8analyze the elasticity impact of the strict LTV limit on each borrower type, given that different LTV cutoffs limit buyer types. Even when controlling for the various LTV cutoffs, I find that investors demonstrate the greatest elasticity in each variable. These results are also encouraging with regard to MPPs: If investors have the greatest impact on housing prices during a housing boom (Kuttner and Shim 2016), MPPs will not prevent them from entering the credit and housing markets, but it will affect their housing choices.Appendix Fshows that the main effects of the strict LTV limit are still large and significant while accounting for seasonality.Table A.5examines the same period before and after the strict LTV limit (January–May 2012 vs. January–May 2013) and demonstrates that the effect is still significant but less pronounced than the initial estimate.

A major concern with the strict LTV limit identification method is that the treatment is predicted using observables (age and income). Consequently, any shock to the credit or housing market behavior that is correlated with these observables will bias the estimated effect of the policy. Changes in interest rates or inflation could be examples of such shocks around the time of the strict LTV limit, affecting not only the outcome variables (credit and housing choices), but also wealth and income. Therefore, I examine various macroeconomic variables around the time of the strict LTV limit implementation, including fluctuations in inflation, unemployment, and mortgage rates (seeAppendix JandFigure A.7). However, these indicators did not significantly change ten months before and six months after the strict LTV limit was implemented (and, separately, for the soft LTV limit as well). As depicted inFigure 1, this paper focuses on brief periods before and after each LTV limit in order to distinguish the specific LTV limit effects from other MPP tools implemented around the same time and other macroeconomic events that occurred around the time of the macroprudential policies, such as the social protest in the summer of 2011.

InTables 8and9, the effect of a strict LTV limit on new contracts in the credit market is shown to have counterintuitive results. The LTV limit should reduce the LTV ratio, thereby reducing the loan’s risk and resulting in a lower interest rate and a shorter term to maturity. However, there is an increase in the interest rate and term to maturity for each buyer type. In Section 6, possible explanations for these results are discussed. In accordance with the literature (Acharya et al. 2022;De Araujo, Barroso, and Gonzalez 2020;Van Bekkum et al. 2019), the results demonstrate a significant reduction in the default rates of treated borrowers relative to those of the control group among first-time homebuyers and upgraders. The decline in default rates does not fully reflect the supply-side response to a change in the LTV requirements, which makes lower (compared to pre-policy) LTV ratios more attractive to issue given the banking system’s limited total equity capital. As documented byCampbell, Ramadorai, and Ranish 2015andBenetton (2021), a portion of the movement in the LTV distribution may be attributable to the supply side, which may also be the result of specialization among lenders. However, one of the major limitations of the data in this paper is the inability to use any lender-side information. Consequently, any question pertaining to the banking system, including competition between banks and pricing responses to LTV limits, could not be included in the current paper.

Tables 8and9demonstrate that borrowers opt for longer-term loans, which would carry higher interest rates even in the absence of policy intervention. However, because the structure of the mortgage market in Israel during this period is fixed across product types, I believe that the results are attributable primarily to the strict LTV limit and not to loan product substitution. In May of 2011, the Bank of Israel implemented a “Variable Interest Rate” tool, which limited the variable interest rate portion of home loans to one-third of the total loan amount. This macroprudential policy tool changed the structure of the mortgage contract terms in Israel. On average, after the 2011 tool, borrowers allocated one-third of the total loan to a component with an adjustable interest rate, one-third to a component with a fixed interest rate, and one-third to a component with a combination of fixed and adjustable interest rates (an adjustable interest rate that is fixed for five years).20
article.6 Additional Analysis on the Effectivene:
Section 5 shows that imposing a strict LTV limit yielded counterintuitive results in the credit market. Although a strict LTV limit was intended to reduce borrower risk and improve credit conditions, affected borrowers paid a higher interest rate and extended the length of their loans. Similar results were found byDe Araujo, Barroso, and Gonzalez 2020, who explained that banks may have altered their risk perception in response to the macroprudential supervisory authority’s strong signal about a buildup of systemic vulnerability among highly leveraged borrowers, which affected their risk pricing. This study provides an additional potential explanation: Due to the LTV limit, affected borrowers may have borrowed money from other sources or utilized nonregulated credit (e.g., consumer credit), resulting in higher monthly loan payments. As a result, to maintain the same monthly payment, affected borrowers had to extend the term to maturity, thereby increasing the mortgage interest rate.

After the imposition of a strict LTV limit, borrowers who still want to purchase a home but lack the equity to do so may use funds from alternative sources. First, they can borrow money from their family (cheaper than credit from other sources), but I assume they have already borrowed as much as possible from family members prior to deciding to purchase a home. Therefore, limiting their LTV ratio should have little impact on their ability to raise additional funds from family in the short term.21Second, borrowers can withdraw funds from their liquid assets. Before deciding to purchase a home, I assume they have already determined how much they wish to spend from their financial resources (this is especially relevant for first-time homebuyers).Figure 9depicts withdrawals from various financial resources prior to and after the LTV limit was imposed, with no significant change in withdrawal amounts after the limit was imposed. Third, borrowers may still purchase the same property they desired prior to the limit, but they must use unregulated consumer credit. There are currently no loan-level data on consumer credit in Israel. In the absence of such data, I use macro-level data and a few loan-level data indicators to examine if there is a shift in demand for unregulated credit following the LTV limit.

Initially, I estimate the decrease in mortgages following the LTV limit. For each borrower type, I calculate the number of mortgages that exceeded the cutoff prior to the policy intervention and find at a figure of approximately 10.3 billion NIS.22However, some of those borrowers did not exit the market; instead, they reduced the loan amount. Using the method described in Subsection 4.2, I match households that were expected to be above the cutoff with their actual LTV choices and analyze the change in loan amount. After the intervention, borrowers with an LTV ratio above the pre-intervention threshold borrowed a total of $3.6 billion NIS. Thus, approximately 6.7 billion NIS was excluded from the mortgage market (representing 15% of the annual amount of mortgages in Israel in 2013), compared with 2.4 billion NIS under the soft LTV limit. Some borrowers acquired less-costly assets. After the limitation, the value of properties acquired by affected borrowers decreased by 3.2 billion NIS. The findings indicate that some borrowers continued to purchase comparable homes with lower mortgages. Simultaneously, housing prices increased 6% and the number of real estate transactions and the number of borrowers did not change. How did borrowers raise this additional amount of money in such a short time?

The average duration of a consumer credit loan in Israel is approximately 4.5 years, compared to 22 years for mortgages. Hence, it increases the borrower’s debt-to-income ratio, which increases the likelihood of default. Thus, in order to maintain the same monthly payment, the affected borrowers had to extend the term to maturity, thereby increasing the mortgage interest rate. Therefore, the strict LTV limit may pose a greater threat to the financial system because it encourages borrowers to utilize risky, expensive, and unregulated credit.
article.7 Conclusion:
Since the global financial crisis of 2008, MPPs have garnered a great deal of attention, and the literature on this topic has expanded rapidly. Despite the importance of the widespread use of MPPs in numerous countries, including Israel, the literature lacks information regarding the benefits and costs of such policies.

This paper estimates the effects of LTV limits on the credit and housing markets’ borrowers’ choices. I find that LTV limits cause borrowers to move to houses that are less expensive, smaller, farther away from the population center, and situated in neighborhoods with a lower socioeconomic ranking. The two limitations—namely, soft and strict LTV limits—have comparable effects on household choices. In terms of magnitude, however, the stricter limit yields greater results, likely due to the higher costs associated with circumventing a strict limit—namely, borrowing unregulated credit. Despite a lack of data on consumer credit that may limit generalization of the findings, this study suggests that future LTV measures should consider non-bank sources of credit as part of the limitations.

This study raises some intriguing but unanswered questions, primarily regarding the welfare implications of borrowers relocating farther from the city center to lower socioeconomic neighborhoods and the general equilibrium implications of LTV limits on housing supply. These are fruitful research topics that I intend to pursue in the future.

Appendix A Merging Mortgages with the Real Estate Database

The mortgage file is merged with the CARMAN file using the following fields: transaction date, transaction price, property location, and block and parcel numbers (Table A.1).23The recording of the block and parcel numbers in the mortgage file is flawed, as 36% of the records are blank and others contain only partial information. Therefore, the block and parcel number field is utilized only if none of the other fields can be modified. The first step in merging the files is a full matching using all three fields; a match is found in about 65,000 records (step 1). When the mortgage file contains multiple matches, the block-parcel field is also utilized, resulting in the identification of 2,000 additional observations (step 2). Occasionally, the transaction registration date in the CARMAN file is distorted. In cases where the mortgages file contains a blank date field and one match to city and price, 500 matched observations are obtained (step 3). A total of 4,000 observations is obtained when the match is incomplete, and a unique match is possible using the block-parcel field (step 4). When the mortgages file and the CARMAN file have incompatible dates (a discrepancy of up to 20 days), but a match exists using the block-parcel field, 2,290 observations are obtained (step 5). When there is no block or parcel number but a single adjustment in the date range of up to five days, 160 paired observations are obtained (step 6). Finally, 14,600 observations are obtained if the date and city match, but the price range is up to 100,000 NIS and a match exists using the block-parcel field (step 7). When locality and date are matched with a gap of up to 1,000 NIS, we obtained 400-paired observations (step 8). In instances where there is a price adjustment, a match in the date and block-parcel fields, but no information on the city, 40 observations are obtained (step 9). In cases where there is no unique detection and the block-parcel field provides a unique identifier, 23 paired observations are obtained (step 10).

Appendix C Changes in Interest Rate for the Affected Borrowers, Event Study Methodology

I also take a quasi-experimental approach based on event studies surrounding the implementation of the soft LTV limit between two types of borrowers: a borrower with LTV above the 60% LTV threshold (“leveraged borrower”) and a borrower with LTV just below the threshold (“nonleveraged borrower”).24For each observation in the data (i.e., loan origination), I denote by t=0 the month of the implementation and index all months relative to that specific month. The baseline specification takes into account a balanced of borrowers whom I observe every month between 10 months before the soft limit and 7 months after; thus, event time t runs from –9 to + 7. I investigate the evolution of the mortgage average interest rate as a function of the event time. Specifically, denoting byyimtlthe interest rate for individual i with leverage l of in month m at event time t, I run the following regression separately for leveraged borrowers and nonleverage borrowers:

𝟙 β 𝟙 γ 𝟙 y imt l = ∑ j ≠ 0 a j l × 1 [ j = t ] + ∑ k β k l × 1 [ k = loa n i s ] + ∑ y γ y l × 1 [ y = m ] + v imt l (A.1)

containing a comprehensive set of event time dummies (first term on the right-hand side), loan and borrower characteristic dummies (second term), and month dummies (third term). Loan and borrower characteristics include the borrower’s age and income, the lending institution, and the loan’s duration. I omit the event time dummy at t=0, indicating that the event time coefficients measure the impact of the LTV limit relative to the month in which the LTV limit was implemented. By including a complete set of loans and borrower characteristic dummies, I control nonparametrically the underlying differences between individuals, and by including a complete set of time dummies, I control nonparametrically the time trends, including monetary policy and other macroeconomic events.

Figure A.2demonstrates that, after loan and borrower characteristics and time trends are accounted for, the interest rates of loans with an LTV ratio of 61% and loans with an LTV ratio of 59% move in tandem until the implementation of the soft LTV limit (panel A). However, at the precise moment that the soft LTV limit is implemented, the interest rate on a loan with an LTV of 61% diverges and experiences an immediate increase in the average interest rate of 0.2 pp, whereas the interest rate on a loan with an LTV of 59% experiences no discernible change. The average interest rate increases by an additional 0.3 pp in the subsequent month. Similar results are shown in panel B, comparing the 61–65% LTV ratio loans to the 55–59% LTV ratio loans.

Appendix F Seasonality and Placebo Test

Due to the seasonality of real estate markets, I chose January-May 2010 as the pre-policy period and compared with January–May 2011 as the post-policy period. Appendix G Effect of LTV Limit on Loan Volumes Using Bunching

Here, I use the bunching methodology to estimate the effect of the soft LTV limit on mortgage amount. Using methods from the bunching literature (Kleven 2016;Best and Kleven 2018;Best et al. 2020), I use the distribution of LTV ratios before and after the limitation to construct counterfactual LTV distributions. BunchingB^is defined as the amount of excess loans with LTV ratios between the minimum bunching levelb_and the maximum bunching level B. Formally,

B ^ = ∑ b _ B ( n b , t − n ^ b , t ) (A.2)

M^represents the number of missing loans with LTV ratios greater than the maximum bunching level. Formally,

M ^ = ∑ B + 1 b max ( n ^ b , t − n b , t ) (A.3)

I use the 2010 distribution as the pre-reform baseline, and the 2011 distribution as the post-reform outcome in this analysis. On the basis of the empirical distributions of LTV ratio in 2009 and 2010, I set the minimum affected LTV ratio parameter 10 pp below the bunching parameter—that is,b_=50⁠. Consequently, the counterfactual LTV distribution in 2011 is given byn^b,2011=π^b,2011×N50,2011⁠. As shown inFigure A.4, which compares the counterfactual distribution to the observed distribution, the observed distribution has a greater proportion of loans with LTV ratios just below 60%. The overall bunching relative to potentially affected loans (⁠B^N^b+1,t⁠) equals to 4.7% with a 95% confidence interval of [0.7,7.6]. The standard errors are estimated using a parametric bootstrap procedure, as inChetty et al. (2011).Figure A.5illustrates a robustness test that estimates the counterfactual LTV distribution in 2009, prior to the limitation. This effect is estimated to be less than 1.8% and is not statistically significant at the 95% confidence level.

Figure A.6plots the counterfactual distribution for the strict LTV limit alongside the observed distribution. The observed distribution has more loans with LTV ratios around the three different LTV limits: 75, 70, and 50.

Appendix H Effect of LTV Limit on Housing Choices, by Buyer Type

The sample is divided into two groups based on the average age of borrowers: young26(up to age 40) versus older adults. Each group undergoes the matching process independently (as depicted inEquation (2)). According toTable A.6, both age groups were affected by the LTV cap, but older adults were affected more than younger adults. Moreover, among older borrowers, real housing purchase prices fell by a significantly greater percentage. In addition, older borrowers decreased the size of the dwellings they purchased and moved significantly further from the city center. These results may be attributable to the possibility that older adults may be more adaptable in their purchasing decisions, allowing them to delay purchases or make concessions regarding the type of asset. In contrast, younger adults may have different limitations and constraints that require them to purchase specific properties in specific locations, such as close to their parents or place of employment.

Appendix J Changes in Macroeconomic Variables Around the LTV Limits

Here, I present changes in various macroeconomic variables a few months before and after each LTV limit to provide evidence that there were no other shocks during this time period. The treatment is predicted based on observables, which is a concern with the strict LTV limit identification strategy (age and income). Therefore, any shock to housing or credit outcomes that is correlated with these observables will bias the estimated effect of the policy. The assumption is that age and income influence outcomes only via LTV limits. To support this claim, I will demonstrate that similar shocks did not occur around the time of the LTV limits.Figure 1also reveals that no additional macroprudential policies were implemented concurrently with the soft and strict LTV limits.
